steps.title: Identify the structures on each side of the blank
steps.content:
The left side completes with "For centuries, scholars dreamed about locating a copy of this legendary lost work" — an independent clause.

The right side is "That fantasy (partially) became reality in 2022, when researchers uncovered traces of the star catalog on a palimpsest" — also an independent clause, where "That" is a demonstrative pronoun pointing back to the dream.

steps.title: Apply the rule for joining independent clauses and eliminate the others
steps.content:
Two independent clauses must be joined with a period (separate sentences), a semicolon, or a comma plus a coordinating conjunction. A period (the remaining alternative) cleanly separates the long-standing dream from the 2022 breakthrough.
  • "work, that": a comma alone cannot join two independent clauses — this is a comma splice. ✗
  • "work that": with no punctuation, "that" tries to function as a relative pronoun making "fantasy…reality" a restrictive clause modifying "work," which is nonsensical; the two independent clauses end up fused with no boundary — a run-on. ✗
  • "work. That": the period properly separates the two independent clauses, and the capitalized demonstrative "That" correctly opens the new sentence. ✓
  • "work and that": joining two independent clauses with a coordinating conjunction requires a comma before "and"; without one, this is a run-on, and the added demonstrative "that" is redundant after the conjunction. ✗
